factual-accuracy.requirement: Explicitly states no published head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy studies exist as of query date
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states multiple times that as of May 2026 there are no published direct head-to-head empirical studies comparing DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy (e.g., in the Introduction and Section B/G).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 requires all 3 social-communication subdomains (social-emotional reciprocity, nonverbal behaviors, relationships)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that DSM-5 Criterion A has three sub-criteria and that “all three must be present,” and reiterates that DSM-5-TR requires deficits “as manifested by all of the following,” listing reciprocity, nonverbal behaviors, and relationships.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 uses examples-based approach for social communication/reciprocity rather than fixed checklist
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes ICD-11 as having a flexible, non-counting structure (e.g., “broad description with no fixed number of features required” and “flexibility allows clinicians…clinical judgment”) and repeatedly notes ICD-11 uses example lists (e.g., sensory examples). This conveys an examples-based approach rather than a fixed checklist for social reciprocity/communication.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 requires at least 2 of 4 restricted and repetitive behavior categories and explicitly includes hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input as one category.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that DSM-5 requires “only 2 of 4 B criteria must be met” and identifies B4 as “Hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input,” explicitly listing it as one of the four restricted/repetitive behavior categories.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 includes sensory reactivity within RRB domain as examples without numeric threshold
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that ICD-11 includes “atypical responses to sensory stimuli” within the restricted/repetitive behaviors domain and provides examples (e.g., hypersensitivity/hyposensitivity to sounds, light, textures, odours, tastes, unusual sensory interests). It presents this as illustrative examples and does not introduce any numeric threshold for sensory reactivity.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 uses three severity levels (Level 1, 2, 3) based on support needed
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states DSM-5 has “3 Levels of Support” and lists Level 1, Level 2, and Level 3 with descriptions framed around “requiring support/substantial support/very substantial support,” which indicates severity levels based on support needed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 uses subcategories by intellectual development and language impairment (codes 6A02.0-6A02.5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly lists ICD-11 autism spectrum disorder subcategories based on intellectual development and functional language, naming codes 6A02.0 through 6A02.5 in the “Severity Specifiers” table.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both require symptoms present in early developmental period but may manifest later when demands exceed capacities or be masked
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states DSM-5 symptoms must be present in the early developmental period but may not become fully manifest until demands exceed capacities or be masked by learned strategies, and it also states ICD-11 onset occurs during the developmental period with symptoms possibly not fully manifest until later when demands exceed capacities (and notes compensation/masking).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both require clinically significant impairment in social, occupational, or other important functioning areas
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states DSM-5 Criterion D requires “clinically significant impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of current functioning” and that ICD-11 requires “significant impairment in personal, family, social, educational, occupational or other important areas of functioning,” indicating both manuals require clinically significant functional impairment.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Kulage et al. 2019 (or similar 2019 meta-analysis) showing approximately 20-21% decrease in DSM-5 diagnoses vs DSM-IV-TR
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response cites a 2019 follow-up systematic review/meta-analysis reporting a 20.8% decrease in ASD diagnoses under DSM-5 vs DSM-IV-TR, matching the ~20–21% figure. However, it does not cite “Kulage et al. 2019” specifically; it provides a different 2019 meta-analysis entry.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Includes at least 1 systematic review or meta-analysis with study design, sample size, and pooled statistics specified
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses (e.g., Kulage et al. 2014; Bennett & Goodall 2016; 2019 follow-up meta-analysis) and specifies study design, sample size (number of included studies), and pooled statistics (e.g., % decrease with 95% CIs and p-values).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Includes at least 1 primary empirical study (e.g., Wiggins et al. 2019 SEED2 n=1,061 or similar) with sample size and sensitivity/specificity data
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes at least one primary empirical study with sample size and sensitivity/specificity data, e.g., Frazier et al. (2012) (n=14,744; sensitivity 0.81, specificity 0.97) and McPartland et al. (2012) (n=933; specificity 94.9% with subgroup sensitivities).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports GRADE quality ratings (High/Moderate/Low/Very Low) for at least 2 sources with justification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response assigns GRADE ratings with brief justifications to multiple sources (e.g., Kulage et al. 2014 “Moderate,” Bennett & Goodall 2016 “Moderate,” Frazier et al. 2012 “Moderate to High,” McPartland et al. 2012 “Low to Moderate”). These include explicit quality labels (High/Moderate/Low) and rationale such as heterogeneity, retrospective design, and lack of ICD-11 comparison.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes available evidence compares DSM-5 vs DSM-IV or discusses ICD-11 conceptually rather than head-to-head testing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that no head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 empirical comparisons exist and instead synthesizes evidence largely from DSM-5 vs DSM-IV studies, plus conceptual ICD-11 discussions (e.g., Greaves-Lord et al. 2023; Kamp-Becker 2024). This satisfies the criterion by characterizing the available evidence as DSM-5 vs DSM-IV and conceptual ICD-11 rather than direct testing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO released ICD-11 CDDR in March 2024 with ASD code 6A02
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses ICD-11 ASD code 6A02 but does not state that WHO released the ICD-11 Clinical Descriptions and Diagnostic Guidelines (CDDG) in March 2024. It instead notes ICD-11 endorsement in May 2019 and effective date January 1, 2022, without any March 2024 CDDG release claim.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO ICD-11 includes atypical sensory reactivity within restricted/inflexible RRB domain
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that ICD-11 includes “atypical responses to sensory stimuli” under the restricted, repetitive, and inflexible patterns of behaviour/interests/activities domain, quoting ICD-11 wording and placing it within the RRB criteria section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States APA DSM-5 requires all 3 social-communication subdomains and at least 2 of 4 RRBs
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that DSM-5 requires deficits in all three social-communication criteria and that at least two of four restricted/repetitive behavior criteria must be met (including in the Autism Speaks quote and the conclusion). This matches the required DSM-5 counting rule.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States AAP 2020 clinical report references DSM-5 for US practice
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the AAP 2020 clinical report is based on and references DSM-5 criteria for US pediatric practice, but it does not explicitly state that the AAP report references DSM-5 specifically “for US practice.” It also does not frame this as an AAP position about US practice standards beyond noting DSM-5-exclusive use.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States AAP notes limited/inconclusive evidence for standalone sensory processing disorder diagnosis
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses sensory processing disorder (SPD) in the context of Autism Speaks and differential diagnosis, but it does not state that the American Academy of Pediatrics notes limited or inconclusive evidence for SPD as a standalone diagnosis. It also does not attribute any such statement to an AAP policy statement or clinical report. Therefore the required AAP position is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NICE or NHS England 2024 framework specifies ICD-11 criteria for primary clinical description
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses NICE guidelines and NHS England coding standards but does not state that a 2024 NICE or NHS England framework specifies ICD-11 criteria for a primary clinical description. It mentions NICE surveillance and NHS England ICD-10 coding standards without any explicit 2024 framework statement about ICD-11 criteria being the primary clinical description.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NHS requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during transition period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses NHS use of ICD-10 morbidity coding and mentions the UK is planning a transition to ICD-11, but it does not state that the NHS requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during the transition period. It also does not mention any NHS mandate tying ICD-10 mapping specifically to national datasets while transitioning.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ASAN supported inclusive unified spectrum diagnosis accounting for masking and compensation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that ASAN supported an inclusive unified spectrum diagnosis accounting for masking and compensation. It instead says no explicit ASAN position comparing DSM-5 vs ICD-11 was found and only offers an inferred view, without attributing support for that specific inclusive framing to ASAN.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States National Autistic Society presents both DSM-5 and ICD-11 as largely similar regarding core characteristics
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the National Autistic Society says “The DSM-5 and ICD-11 reference the ‘core characteristics’ of autism,” and frames them as equally valid options under NICE guidance. This presents DSM-5 and ICD-11 as largely similar regarding core characteristics.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NAS uses identity-affirming or neurodiversity-affirming language
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response quotes the National Autistic Society using identity-first language (e.g., “autistic people,” “autistic individuals”) and frames sensory differences as “sensory processing differences,” which is consistent with identity-affirming/neurodiversity-affirming language.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Autism Speaks provides DSM-5 text but does not take formal position on DSM vs ICD preference
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says Autism Speaks has informational content about DSM-5 criteria and that it has not taken a public position comparing DSM-5 vs ICD-11. However, it does not state that Autism Speaks provides DSM-5 text while not taking a formal DSM vs ICD preference position, so that specific combination is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States meta-analysis or empirical evidence shows DSM-5 resulted in approximately 20-21% decrease in diagnoses vs DSM-IV-TR
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports a 20.8% decrease in ASD diagnoses under DSM-5 vs DSM-IV-TR ("Five-Year Follow-Up Meta-Analysis (2019)") and also gives other decrease estimates (e.g., 22% for Autistic Disorder). This constitutes meta-analytic evidence of an approximately 20–21% decrease.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes DSM-5 showed reduced sensitivity for PDD-NOS, Asperger profiles, or cognitively able subgroups
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes DSM-5 reduced sensitivity for Asperger’s and PDD-NOS profiles and for cognitively able subgroups, citing McPartland et al. (2012) with low sensitivity for Asperger’s (0.25) and PDD-NOS (0.28) and lower sensitivity for IQ ≥ 70 (0.46).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes ICD-11 breadth and compensation language may increase false positives or over-diagnosis (concern awaiting empirical testing)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly warns that ICD-11’s broader, more flexible criteria may increase false positives/overdiagnosis and repeatedly frames this as a conceptual concern that “requires direct empirical validation” or is “awaiting empirical testing,” citing Kamp-Becker (2024) and labeling it theoretical.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States sensory features have high sensitivity but lower specificity for ASD (occur in other neurodevelopmental conditions)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that sensory features have poor specificity (e.g., Grapel et al. 2015 specificity 0.34) and states that sensory abnormalities are frequent in other disorders such as ADHD and SPD, implying they occur across neurodevelopmental conditions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States US uses ICD-10-CM code F84.0 for autism spectrum disorder
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states in Section F.1 that the United States uses ICD-10-CM for billing and identifies “ICD-10-CM F84.0” as the primary autism code, describing it as including autism spectrum disorder.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States US has no official ICD-11-CM implementation date or transition timeline announced
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states in Section F.1 that the United States has not adopted ICD-11 for billing and that “No official implementation date has been set for ICD-11 morbidity coding in the US,” indicating no official implementation date or transition timeline has been announced.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NHS England uses ICD-11 for clinical description of autism, noting that the transition from ICD-10 to ICD-11 is underway.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses ICD-11 transition planning in the UK/NHS but does not state that NHS England uses ICD-11 for the clinical description of autism. It also does not explicitly note that NHS England’s transition from ICD-10 to ICD-11 is underway in the context of autism clinical description.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States UK still requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during transition
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the UK NHS is still using ICD-10 for morbidity coding and notes no specific ICD-11 implementation date was found, implying ICD-10 mapping remains required during the transition. It does not explicitly use the phrase “requires ICD-10 mapping,” but the described ongoing ICD-10 coding standard and transition planning satisfy the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO ICD-11 took effect January 1, 2022 for member states
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that ICD-11 “came into effect on January 1, 2022” and reiterates that it “came into effect globally on January 1, 2022” for WHO implementation, which covers the effective date for member states.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly flags absence of direct DSM-5 vs ICD-11 studies and distinguishes projections/inferences from empirical data
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly and explicitly states that no direct head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 empirical comparison studies or systematic reviews exist (e.g., in the Introduction, Section B “Critical Note,” and Section G). It also labels claims as “EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE” versus “CONCEPTUAL CONCERN” and flags inferred positions as inference, distinguishing projections from data.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Quantifies diagnostic impact by subgroup (e.g., ~21% decrease overall, differential PDD-NOS/Asperger/cognitively able outcomes)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response quantifies diagnostic impact with meta-analytic decreases (e.g., 31% overall, 20.8% overall) and provides differential subgroup effects for PDD-NOS and Asperger’s (e.g., large reductions/retention rates) and cognitively able/higher-IQ individuals (lower sensitivity). These satisfy the requested subgroup quantification example.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Contrasts DSM-5 numeric RRB threshold (≥2 of 4) vs ICD-11 clinical judgment approach without fixed count
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states DSM-5 requires “at least two of four” restricted/repetitive behavior criteria (implying the ≥2-of-4 numeric threshold) and contrasts this with ICD-11’s “flexible approach” and “clinical judgment” with “no fixed number of features required.” This directly contrasts fixed counting vs no fixed count.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes test accuracy studies from clinical utility perspectives and provides GRADE ratings with justification
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response separates empirical diagnostic accuracy evidence (e.g., sensitivity/specificity studies and DSM-5 vs DSM-IV meta-analyses) from conceptual/clinical-utility commentary (explicitly labeled as conceptual concerns). It also assigns GRADE ratings (e.g., Moderate, Low–Moderate, Moderate–High) for multiple sources and provides brief justifications such as heterogeneity, retrospective design, and lack of direct ICD-11 comparisons.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Integrates sensory role, functional impairment, and social-communication + RRB dyad into actionable clinical decision guidance
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links sensory features (as part of RRBs), functional impairment thresholds, and the social-communication + RRB dyad, and it translates these differences into clinical decision guidance (e.g., underdiagnosis vs overdiagnosis risk profiles and differential diagnosis cautions for sensory-dominant presentations). It provides actionable implications such as needing broader differential diagnosis under ICD-11 and vigilance for masking/atypical presentations under DSM-5.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains disconnect between clinical ICD-11 adoption and administrative ICD-10 mapping/coding during transition
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly describes a transition gap where clinical criteria may be DSM-5/ICD-11 while administrative billing remains ICD-10 (e.g., US uses ICD-10-CM F84.0 and has not adopted ICD-11 for billing, with mapping/training challenges). It notes that adoption of ICD-11 criteria does not currently affect reimbursement because coding systems haven’t transitioned, explaining the disconnect.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies ICD-11 breadth trading sensitivity for specificity as hypothesis requiring empirical validation rather than settled fact
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly frames ICD-11’s broader, more permissive criteria as a theoretical concern/hypothesis, repeatedly noting the lack of head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 accuracy studies and stating that implications for specificity/false positives “remain hypotheses requiring direct empirical validation.”

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States DSM-5 and ICD-11 are essentially identical or interchangeable without acknowledging differences in RRB thresholds or severity systems
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly notes that DSM-5 and ICD-11 have “converged significantly” and that ICD-11 “mirrors” DSM-5, but it also explicitly describes key differences, including DSM-5’s requirement for all three social-communication criteria and different severity/specifier systems. It also discusses differing diagnostic thresholds and risk profiles, so it does not present them as interchangeable without acknowledging differences.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes response with six lettered sections (A through F) addressing each query component as specified
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into lettered sections A through F (and additionally includes a Section G), with each of A–F corresponding to the query’s requested components.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents DSM-5 vs ICD-11 criteria comparison in a table or structured side-by-side format for Section A
presentation-quality.explanation: Section A includes multiple side-by-side tables comparing DSM-5 and ICD-11, including a dedicated table for sensory processing mention/weighting and other required rows (severity, onset, impairment). This satisfies the requirement for a structured DSM-5 vs ICD-11 comparison format for Section A.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise diagnostic and psychometric terminology (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, GRADE, diagnostic criteria)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses precise diagnostic and psychometric terminology, including sensitivity, specificity, PPV/NPV, AUC, ROC curve analysis, false positives/false negatives, and GRADE quality ratings. It also references diagnostic criteria structure (e.g., polythetic thresholds, specifiers) in technical terms.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains scholarly, evidence-based tone distinguishing empirical findings from clinical inference or opinion
presentation-quality.explanation: The response generally uses a formal, academic tone and repeatedly labels claims as empirical evidence versus conceptual concern/inference, explicitly noting evidence gaps and avoiding overstatement. It distinguishes direct quotations, study findings, and hypotheses, and flags when positions are inferred rather than documented.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents DSM-5 vs ICD-11 criteria comparison as prose paragraphs without table or structured side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple side-by-side comparison tables (e.g., DSM-5 vs ICD-11 criteria, sensory processing, severity, onset, impairment) rather than presenting the comparison only as prose paragraphs. While it includes narrative sections, it is not exclusively unstructured prose.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing (e.g., 'sensory stuff', 'pretty similar', 'basically the same') instead of clinical terminology
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently uses formal clinical terminology (e.g., “restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior,” “hyper- or hyporeactivity,” “diagnostic threshold,” “sensitivity/specificity”) and does not use colloquial phrases like “sensory stuff,” “pretty similar,” or “basically the same.”

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official DSM-5 sources from APA, CDC, or cdc.gov/ncbddd/autism/hcp-dsm.html
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites an official APA source for DSM-5-TR changes via an APA-hosted PDF (psychiatry.org). It does not cite any CDC or the specific CDC DSM-5 autism criteria page, but the APA citation satisfies the criterion.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official ICD-11 sources from WHO CDDR (who.int) or pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov for ICD-11 clinical descriptions
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites ICD-11 clinical description content using a PMC-hosted peer-reviewed article (Greaves-Lord et al. 2023) and also includes WHO ICD-11 adoption information via a who.int news page. These satisfy the requirement to cite official WHO (who.int) or PMC (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) sources for ICD-11 clinical descriptions.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites peer-reviewed medical/psychiatric journals via PubMed (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov), PMC, BMC Medicine, Molecular Psychiatry, or similar for evidence synthesis
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple peer-reviewed sources with PubMed and PMC links (e.g., Kulage et al. 2014 on pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov and several articles on pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) as part of its evidence synthesis.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites WHO ICD-11 implementation materials or clinical guidance from who.int
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites WHO materials hosted on who.int, including the WHO news item “World Health Assembly Endorses ICD-11” (https://www.who.int/news/item/25-05-2019-world-health-assembly-update), which is an ICD-11 implementation-related WHO source.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 of NICE (nice.org.uk), AAP (publications.aap.org), NHS England (england.nhs.uk) for organizational positions
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites AAP via the Pediatrics clinical report and AAP page (e.g., source [23]/[24]) and cites NICE guidance pages (e.g., sources [25]-[29]) to describe organizational guideline positions.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 of ASAN (autisticadvocacy.org), National Autistic Society (autism.org.uk), Autism Speaks for advocacy perspectives
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites ASAN via autisticadvocacy.org (e.g., sources [31], [32], [34]) and also cites the National Autistic Society via autism.org.uk (e.g., sources [30], [35], [36]). It additionally cites Autism Speaks (e.g., sources [1], [37], [38]).

citation-quality.requirement: For evidence synthesis sources (systematic reviews, meta-analyses, primary studies), uses publications dated 2013 or later per query requirement
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple evidence synthesis sources published before 2013 (e.g., Frazier et al. 2012 and McPartland et al. 2012) and relies on them for diagnostic accuracy statistics. Because the criterion requires evidence synthesis sources to be dated 2013 or later, this requirement is not satisfied.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Reddit, patient forums, WebMD comments, or social media posts as sources for diagnostic criteria or clinical evidence
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite Reddit, patient forums, WebMD comments, or social media posts as sources for diagnostic criteria or clinical evidence. All cited sources are organizational websites, journals, government/WHO/NICE pages, or other non-social-media outlets.
